items.title: Subject-Verb Agreement: The Rule Every Exam Tests First
items.summary: Master the core rules of subject-verb agreement, including tricky cases like collective nouns, indefinite pronouns, and subjects separated from verbs by long phrases.
items.body:
Subject-verb agreement means the verb must match its subject in number. A singular subject takes a singular verb, and a plural subject takes a plural verb. The dog barks at strangers. The dogs bark at strangers. This sounds simple, but examiners design questions where the true subject is hidden or disguised.

The most common trap is a phrase placed between the subject and the verb. In the sentence, the box of mangoes was kept in the kitchen, the subject is box, not mangoes, so the singular verb was is correct. Always strike out prepositional phrases beginning with of, along with, as well as, together with, and in addition to before deciding the verb. The captain, along with his teammates, was honoured at the ceremony.

Indefinite pronouns such as each, every, either, neither, anyone, everyone, somebody, and nobody are singular. Each of the students has submitted the assignment. Neither of the answers is correct. With either-or and neither-nor constructions, the verb agrees with the subject nearer to it. Neither the manager nor the clerks were present. Neither the clerks nor the manager was present.

Collective nouns like team, committee, jury, and family take a singular verb when the group acts as one unit. The committee has approved the proposal. They take a plural verb when members act individually. The jury were divided in their opinions. Some nouns look plural but are singular in meaning, such as news, physics, mathematics, and politics. The news is encouraging. Conversely, nouns like scissors, trousers, and spectacles take plural verbs. These trousers are too long.

Amounts of money, time, and distance usually take singular verbs because they express one total quantity. Five hundred rupees is a fair price. Ten kilometres is a long walk. Titles of books and names of organisations are also singular. Gulliver's Travels is a classic.

Quick practice tip: whenever you face an agreement question, do three things in order. First, find the verb. Second, ask who or what performs that action and underline only that word. Third, ignore every phrase between the subject and the verb. Practise this three-step drill on ten sentences daily for a week, and agreement errors in error-spotting questions will start jumping out at you instantly.

items.title: Tense Consistency: Keeping Time Straight in Every Sentence
items.summary: Learn how to maintain consistent tenses within and across sentences, handle reported speech shifts, and spot the unjustified tense changes that exams love to test.
items.body:
Tense consistency means a sentence or passage should not shift between time frames without a logical reason. If you begin describing a past event, stay in the past unless the meaning genuinely demands a change. Incorrect: She opened the door and walks into the room. Correct: She opened the door and walked into the room. Both actions happened in the same past moment, so both verbs must be in the past tense.

Some tense shifts are justified. When a sentence connects a past action to a present result, mixing tenses is natural. He missed the morning train, so he is still waiting at the station. Universal truths stay in the present tense even inside past-tense sentences. The teacher explained that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius. Exams frequently test this rule by wrongly shifting a universal truth into the past.

Reported speech follows a backshift pattern. When the reporting verb is in the past, the tense inside the reported clause usually moves one step back. Direct: He said, I am tired. Indirect: He said that he was tired. Present perfect becomes past perfect. She said, I have finished. She said that she had finished. Remember that the simple past in direct speech becomes past perfect when sequence matters. He said that he had visited Jaipur the previous year.

Watch out for time markers, because they lock a sentence into a tense. Words like yesterday, last year, and ago demand the simple past, never the present perfect. Incorrect: I have met him yesterday. Correct: I met him yesterday. Words like since, for, already, and yet often signal the perfect tenses. She has lived in Pune since 2019. With when-clauses about the past, pair simple past with past continuous or past perfect sensibly. When the phone rang, she was cooking dinner.

In conditional sentences, keep the pairings fixed. If he works hard, he will succeed. If he worked hard, he would succeed. If he had worked hard, he would have succeeded. Mixing these frames, such as writing would have succeeded with if he worked, is a classic error-spotting trap.

Quick practice tip: while solving any sentence, circle every time marker first, then check whether each verb obeys it. Rewrite five faulty sentences daily, correcting only the verbs, and the backshift and pairing rules will become automatic before exam day.

items.title: Articles and Determiners: Choosing A, An, The, or Nothing at All
items.summary: A clear guide to using a, an, the, and zero article correctly, plus determiners like some, any, much, many, few, and little that exams test repeatedly.
items.body:
Articles are small words with big consequences in exams. Use a or an when mentioning something for the first time or referring to any one member of a class. Use the when both the writer and the reader know exactly which thing is meant. I saw a dog in the park. The dog was chasing a squirrel. The first sentence introduces the dog; the second refers back to that specific dog.

The choice between a and an depends on sound, not spelling. Use an before vowel sounds and a before consonant sounds. An hour, an honest man, and an MLA take an because the words begin with vowel sounds. A university, a one-rupee coin, and a European city take a because they begin with consonant sounds like yu and wa. This sound rule is among the most frequently tested points in error-spotting sections.

Use the with things that are unique, such as the sun, the moon, and the Himalayas. Use it with superlatives, ordinal numbers, and well-known landmarks. She is the tallest girl in the class. He was the first person to arrive. Rivers, mountain ranges, oceans, and newspapers usually take the, while names of people, most countries, cities, languages, and meals take no article. We had dinner after we reached Mumbai. Exceptions include country names containing common nouns, such as the United States and the Netherlands.

The zero article also applies to plural and uncountable nouns used in a general sense. Honesty is the best policy. Books are a window to the world. But once you specify, the returns. The honesty of that shopkeeper surprised everyone.

Determiners extend this logic. Many, few, and a few go with countable nouns, while much, little, and a little go with uncountable nouns. Few means almost none, while a few means some. Few students attended, so the hall looked empty. A few students attended, so the discussion was still lively. Some appears in positive sentences and offers, while any appears in negatives and questions. I have some questions. Do you have any doubts?

Quick practice tip: when you meet a noun in any practice sentence, ask two questions. Is it specific or general? Is it countable or uncountable? Answering both before choosing the article or determiner will resolve nearly every exam question on this topic.

items.title: Prepositions in Context: Fixed Pairings You Must Memorise
items.summary: Understand how prepositions of time, place, and direction work, and learn the fixed verb, noun, and adjective pairings that dominate exam questions.
items.body:
Prepositions show the relationship between a noun or pronoun and the rest of the sentence, usually involving time, place, or direction. The trouble is that their usage is often fixed by convention rather than logic, which is exactly why exams test them so heavily.

For time, remember the size rule. Use at for precise points, on for days and dates, and in for longer periods. The meeting starts at nine. The shop is closed on Sunday. She was born in March. The same widening logic works for place. He waited at the bus stop, on Park Street, in Kolkata. Use by for deadlines and within for limits. Submit the form by Friday. Finish the test within two hours. Use since with a starting point and for with a duration. He has worked here since 2020. He has worked here for six years.

Direction prepositions cause subtler errors. Use into for movement toward the inside and in for position. She walked into the room and then sat in a corner. Use between for two items and among for more than two. The property was divided between the two brothers, while the sweets were distributed among the children. Use beside to mean next to and besides to mean in addition to. He sat beside me. Besides English, she speaks Tamil.

The richest source of exam questions is fixed pairings. Certain verbs, nouns, and adjectives demand specific prepositions. We rely on friends, deal with problems, consist of parts, and abstain from sugar. We show an affection for someone, a reason for an action, and a solution to a problem. We are good at mathematics, afraid of failure, capable of improvement, and different from others. Note the pairs that change meaning. Agree with a person but agree to a proposal. Die of a disease but die from an injury.

Also watch for verbs that take no preposition at all. Discuss, attack, resemble, and reach are followed directly by their objects. Incorrect: We discussed about the plan. Correct: We discussed the plan. This single rule appears in error-spotting sections year after year.

Quick practice tip: maintain a running list of preposition pairings in groups of ten. Each day, write one original sentence for every pairing on that day's list instead of just reading it. Producing the phrase yourself fixes it in memory far better than recognition alone.

items.title: Misplaced Modifiers: Why Your Sentence Says the Wrong Thing
items.summary: Learn to spot and fix misplaced and dangling modifiers, the silent errors that make sentences absurd and that sentence-improvement questions test constantly.
items.body:
A modifier is a word or phrase that describes another part of the sentence. The golden rule is simple: place the modifier as close as possible to the word it describes. When a modifier drifts away from its target, the sentence can say something unintended or even ridiculous, and that is precisely what sentence-improvement questions exploit.

Consider this sentence: Running across the field, the school building came into view. Read literally, the school building was running across the field. The opening phrase has nothing sensible to attach to, which makes it a dangling modifier. The fix is to supply the real doer right after the comma. Running across the field, we saw the school building come into view. Always check that the subject immediately after an opening phrase is the person or thing performing that action.

Misplaced single words cause quieter damage. Words like only, even, just, almost, and nearly change meaning depending on position. Only she lent him money means nobody else did. She lent only him money means she lent to nobody else. She lent him only money means she gave money and nothing more. In exams, a sentence with only floating in the wrong place is a frequent improvement question. Place such limiting words directly before the word they restrict.

Misplaced phrases create similar confusion. The shopkeeper sold a bicycle to the customer with a broken bell. As written, the customer has a broken bell. Corrected: The shopkeeper sold the customer a bicycle with a broken bell. Likewise, clauses can dangle. We saw a crowd near the stadium that was shouting slogans. The stadium appears to be shouting. Better: Near the stadium, we saw a crowd that was shouting slogans.

A related trap is the squinting modifier, which sits between two words and could describe either. Students who revise frequently score well. Does frequently describe revising or scoring? Rewrite to remove the ambiguity. Students who frequently revise score well.

Quick practice tip: develop the habit of reading every sentence literally, as if you knew no context. Ask of each descriptive phrase, what exactly is this touching, and is that the thing it should describe? Take five sentences from any practice passage daily, find each modifier, and draw an arrow to its target. If the arrow points to the wrong word, rewrite the sentence. Two weeks of this drill makes modifier errors almost impossible to miss.

items.title: Active vs Passive Voice: Transformation Rules Made Simple
items.summary: A step-by-step method for converting between active and passive voice across tenses, with guidance on when each voice is appropriate and what exams test.
items.body:
Voice tells us whether the subject of a sentence performs the action or receives it. In the active voice, the doer comes first. The gardener watered the plants. In the passive voice, the receiver of the action becomes the subject. The plants were watered by the gardener. Exams test voice in two ways: direct transformation questions and error-spotting items where the verb form is wrong.

The transformation follows a fixed recipe. First, the object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence. Second, insert the correct form of the verb be that matches the tense of the original. Third, change the main verb to its past participle. Fourth, mention the original doer after by, or drop it if it is unimportant or obvious. Someone has stolen my umbrella becomes My umbrella has been stolen, where the doer vanishes because it is unknown.

Learn the be-form for each tense by pattern, not by rote panic. Simple present uses is or are written. Simple past uses was or were written. Present continuous uses is being written. Past continuous uses was being written. Present perfect uses has been or have been written. Simple future uses will be written. Note that future continuous and perfect continuous tenses are not normally used in the passive, a fact that itself appears as a question. Modal verbs follow one pattern: modal plus be plus past participle. The form must be filled. The work can be completed by Monday.

Imperatives take special structures. Shut the door becomes Let the door be shut. Requests often use the form You are requested. Please help the poor becomes You are requested to help the poor. Questions retain their question form. Who wrote this letter becomes By whom was this letter written.

Beyond mechanics, know when each voice is appropriate. Active voice is direct and usually preferred in good writing. Passive voice is right when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or deliberately hidden, or when the action matters more than the agent. The bridge was built in 1932. In comprehension passages, recognising passive constructions also helps you identify who actually did what.

Quick practice tip: take one tense per day and convert ten original sentences both ways, active to passive and back. Say the be-form aloud before writing. Cycling through all the tenses twice gives you complete command of every pattern the exam can ask.

items.title: Error Spotting Decoded: The Patterns Behind Every Question
items.summary: A strategic checklist of the error families that dominate spot-the-error questions, with original examples and a scanning routine you can apply in seconds.
items.body:
Error-spotting questions look unpredictable, but they are built from a small set of recurring patterns. Once you learn to scan for these families in a fixed order, most questions take under thirty seconds.

Start with agreement, the most common family. Check that every verb matches its subject and that every pronoun matches its noun in number and gender. Incorrect: One of my friends are a doctor. Correct: One of my friends is a doctor. Pronoun errors hide in the same way. Incorrect: Every student must bring their own calculator. In formal exam grammar, write his or her, or recast the subject as plural students.

Next, scan the verbs for tense and form errors. Look for time markers that clash with the tense, as in I have visited Delhi last month, which needs the simple past visited. Check for wrong participles, such as he had went, and for missing helping verbs in continuous forms.

Third, inspect small words. Articles, prepositions, and conjunctions carry a large share of errors. Watch for a missing the before superlatives, a wrong preposition after a verb, or an unnecessary one, as in discussed about. Paired conjunctions must stay intact and balanced: not only must pair with but also, hardly with when, no sooner with than, and scarcely with when. No sooner had the bell rung than the students rushed out.

Fourth, check comparisons and parallelism. Comparative forms should not be doubled. Incorrect: more better. Items in a list must share the same grammatical form. Incorrect: She likes singing, dancing, and to paint. Correct: She likes singing, dancing, and painting. Also ensure logical comparison. The climate of Shimla is cooler than that of Delhi, not cooler than Delhi.

Fifth, look at word-level traps. Some nouns are never plural, such as furniture, advice, information, and luggage. Incorrect: She gave me many advices. Adverb and adjective confusion appears too. He played good should be he played well. Finally, watch for redundancy, where two words repeat one idea. Incorrect: return back, repeat again, final conclusion.

Quick practice tip: build a five-point scan and apply it in the same order every time. One, subject and verb. Two, tense against time markers. Three, small words and pairs. Four, comparison and parallelism. Five, word traps and redundancy. Practise the scan on fifteen sentences daily, naming the family of each error you find. Naming the pattern is what converts scattered knowledge into reliable exam speed.
